text: The surgeon incises the peritoneum and proceeds with the dissection by moving posterior to the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and inferior mesenteric vein (IMV). The mesentery of the descending and sigmoid colon is carefully peeled off the retroperitoneum. The ureter is pushed down, while vessels and the colon are elevated, along with Toldt's fascia. The kidney is moved downward, ensuring anatomical separation before dividing the IMA or IMV.

The surgeon uses a tip-up grasper to elevate the recto-sigma junction, jiggling the peritoneum to predict the junction between the mesorectum and retroperitoneum. An incision is made at this junction to enter the avascular plane, with visible nerves and a bridging vessel being pushed down to preserve them. The left ureter is identified as the dissection progresses medially to laterally.

The surgeon uses a tip-up grasper to elevate the recto-sigma junction, preparing for the infra-IMA approach. The peritoneum is jiggled to identify the incision site at the junction between the mesorectum and the retroperitoneum, with the peritoneum visibly covering both structures.

The surgeon manipulates the opaque peritoneum to predict the junction between the mesorectum and retroperitoneum, aiming to make an incision at this junction to enter the avascular plane. As the peritoneum is jiggled, bloody tendrils of nerves are visible, which are pushed down to preserve them. A bridging vessel is also pushed down. The left ureter is identified and comes into view.

The video shows the anatomy of a thin, healthy female patient with an obstructing sigmoid cancer. The transverse colon is visible, with the splenic flexure located superiorly and the descending colon positioned at the top of the screen. The sigmoid colon is located distally off-screen. The duodenal-jejunal junction is visible, with the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) passing below the duodenum. The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is seen entering the fatty para-aortic tissue. The left kidney is visible, and the pancreas is located above the transverse colon.

The video shows the anatomy of a thin, healthy female patient with an obstructing sigmoid cancer. The transverse colon is visible, with the splenic flexure located superiorly and the descending colon positioned at the top of the screen. The sigmoid colon is located distally off-screen. The duodenal-jejunal junction is visible, with the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) running below the duodenum. The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is seen entering the fatty para-aortic tissue. The left kidney is visible, and the pancreas is situated above the transverse colon.

The surgeon begins the infra-IMA approach, noting the altered angle of the IMA due to the tumor encasing the left ureter. The left ureter is visible, squiggling in the retroperitoneum. The surgeon uses a tip-up grasper to hold tissue upwards and circumferentially dissects the IMA distal to its takeoff from the aorta. Firefly imaging is used to identify the ureter with ICG, ensuring a safe margin around the vessel while stripping off peritoneum and lymph nodes.

The surgeon uses a tip-up grasper to elevate tissue in the north direction while circumferentially dissecting the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) immediately distal to its takeoff from the aorta. The left ureter is visible, squiggling in the retroperitoneum, surrounded by areolar tissue. Firefly imaging is used to highlight the ureter with indocyanine green (ICG) due to the presence of an obstructing tumor encasing the ureter.

The surgeon carefully strips the peritoneum and lymph nodes from the vessel, leaving a one to two centimeter cuff. The camera view is adjusted to show the thin descending mesocolon. The surgeon then divides the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) at the duodenal-jejunal junction, ensuring a one centimeter cuff is left. The pancreas is visible above the IMV.

The surgeon lifts the tissue anteriorly and brushes it down, revealing the thin descending mesocolon. The camera view is adjusted to show the duodenal-jejunal junction and the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) below the duodenum. The surgeon divides the IMV with a one-centimeter cuff, with the pancreas visible above. The next step involves taking down the splenic flexure.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, identifying the junction between the yellowish fat of the mesorectum and the paler retroperitoneal fat. An incision is made at this junction, exposing areolar tissue and entering the pre-sacral space. The dissection continues below the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), peeling it off the aorta while carefully tending to the left ureter and hypogastric nerves. The retroperitoneal fat is pushed down, and the surgeon uses passive movements to manipulate the tissue, pulling it back towards the eleven o'clock position on the screen.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, jiggling the peritoneum to identify the junction between the yellowish fat of the mesorectum and the palish, salmony retroperitoneal fat. An incision is made at this junction, exposing areolar tissue and entering the presacral space. The dissection continues below the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), peeling it off the aorta while identifying and preserving the left ureter and hypogastric nerves. The retroperitoneal fat is pushed down, and the surgeon uses their left hand to passively lift and hook tissue, pulling it back towards the eleven o'clock position on the screen.

The surgeon uses a sub-IMV approach, tenting up the inferior mesenteric vein and lifting the mesenteric fat into a twelve o'clock orientation. An incision is made immediately posterior to the IMV, pushing down retroperitoneal attachments to access the avascular plane. The IMV is elevated while critical structures are pushed down, connecting the sub-IMV incision to the supra-IMA space.

The surgeon uses a sub-IMV approach, tenting up the inferior mesenteric vein and lifting the mesenteric fat into a twelve o'clock orientation. The incision is made immediately posterior to the IMV, pushing down retroperitoneal attachments to access the avascular plane. The IMV is elevated while critical structures are pushed down, connecting the sub-IMV incision to the supra-IMA space.

The surgeon skeletonizes the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), creating a T-shaped configuration where the IMA branches from the aorta. The left colic artery is seen heading upwards, while the superior hormonal artery heads downwards. The dissection combines sub-IMA and supra-IMA approaches, revealing the anatomical layout of the IMA and its bifurcations.

The surgeon skeletonizes the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), creating a T-shaped configuration with the aorta below. The left colic artery extends upwards, while the superior hormonal artery descends. The IMA is visible at the junction where it branches from the aorta, forming the base of the T. The dissection combines sub-IMA and supra-IMA approaches, revealing the anatomical layout.

The surgeon performs a bailout maneuver by tenting the recto-sigma junction anteriorly to locate the left ureter from a lateral approach. The IMA pedicle is visible, and the surgeon identifies the bare area above the IMA, incising it to enter the retroperitoneal space. The left ureter is exposed, situated beneath gonadal vessels and retroperitoneal vasculature.

The surgeon tents the recto-sigma junction anteriorly to expose the bare area above the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). Using the left hand, the surgeon incises into the retroperitoneal space, revealing the left ureter beneath gonadal vessels and retroperitoneal vasculature.

The surgical view shows the duodenum on the left side of the screen and the aorta with the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) branching off. The surgeon is tenting the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) anteriorly in a cachectic patient undergoing a total colectomy for ulcerative colitis.

The video shows the duodenum on the left side of the screen, with the aorta visible centrally. The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) is seen branching off the aorta. The surgeon tents the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) anteriorly in a cachectic patient undergoing total colectomy for ulcerative colitis.

The surgeon uses cautery to make an incision immediately posterior to the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), sweeping the mesentery anteriorly to enter the avascular plane. The thin tissue planes are carefully navigated to avoid the mesentery and kidney. The IMV is elevated off the duodenum, and the pancreas is identified at the lower border. A window is created around the IMV for skeletonization and division.

The surgeon uses cautery to make an incision immediately posterior to the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV), sweeping the mesentery anteriorly and entering an avascular plane. The thin tissue planes are visible, and the IMV is elevated off the duodenum. The pancreas is identified at the lower border, and a window is created around the IMV for skeletonization and division.
